coredata.dc:title: White light emission by energy transfer from areca nut husk extract loaded with carbon dots synthesized from the same extract
coredata.dc:creator: Mathew, Jinu
Joy, Josny
Kumar S, Ajeesh
Philip, Jacob
coredata.dc:description: Natural dyes having ability to emit white light have gained great attention in recent years as they have the potential for use as sensors, back-lights and display systems. In this work, luminescence decay and energy transfer in a natural dye extracted from the husk of areca nut, loaded with carbon dots, have been studied for their emission properties. These carbon nanoparticles are prepared from the natural dye of areca nut by a simple and facile hydrothermal treatment. White light emission is achieved from a mixture of areca nut extract and carbon dots prepared from the same extract with good Commission Internationale del’ Eclairage (CIE) index values (0.28, 0.34) on photoexcitation at 380 nm. The areca nut juice is extracted from the husk of the ripened fruit and the carbon nanoparticles prepared have sizes in the range 4–5 nm. These findings suggest auspicious opportunity for using natural dyes with fluorescent nanoparticles for developing white luminescent display systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: White light emission
Luminescence decay
Natural dyes
Carbon dots
Hydrothermal process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: One-step synthesis of carbon dots from hydrothermal treatment of ripened areca nut.
FTIR spectrum of areca nut extract.
Structure of sarcosine N-(2-furoyl) - decyl ester.
UV absorption spectra (a) Areca nut extract (b) Carbon nanoparticles from areca nut extract.
Photoluminescence spectra (a) Areca nut extract (b) Carbon nanoparticles from areca nut extract at different excitation wavelengths.
Photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of carbon dots extracted from areca nut.
TEM images of carbon dots synthesized from areca nut extract.
Raman spectra. (a) Areca nut juice (b) Carbon dots synthesized from areca nut.
CIE color coordinates plot of Areca nut and CDs of Areca nut extract.
Photoluminescence spectra of mixture of Areca nut extract and CDs of areca nut at different compositions.
CIE plot for mixture of Areca nut extract and CDs of areca nut at different compositions.
A spectral overlap of absorption of areca nut extract and emission of areca nut CDs (insight shows Cool WLE by the mixture W).
Time resolved PL decays of white light emitting mixture of areca nut and CDs and areca nut CDs alone.

head.ce:title: White light emission by energy transfer from areca nut husk extract loaded with carbon dots synthesized from the same extract

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Amal Jyothi College of Engineering, Kottayam, Kerala 686518, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, St Joseph Engineering College

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Natural dyes having ability to emit white light have gained great attention in recent years as they have the potential for use as sensors, back-lights and display systems. In this work, luminescence decay and energy transfer in a natural dye extracted from the husk of areca nut, loaded with carbon dots, have been studied for their emission properties. These carbon nanoparticles are prepared from the natural dye of areca nut by a simple and facile hydrothermal treatment. White light emission is achieved from a mixture of areca nut extract and carbon dots prepared from the same extract with good Commission Internationale del’ Eclairage (CIE) index values (0.28, 0.34) on photoexcitation at 380 nm. The areca nut juice is extracted from the husk of the ripened fruit and the carbon nanoparticles prepared have sizes in the range 4–5 nm. These findings suggest auspicious opportunity for using natural dyes with fluorescent nanoparticles for developing white luminescent display systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescence is the emission of light by a material without applying heat on to it. It occurs when a relevant material absorbs energy from high energy sources like X- rays or ultraviolet radiation, chemical reactions and so on. This absorbed energy drives the atoms of the material in to their excited states. Atoms in the excited state are not stable there (on account of quantum mechanical selection rules) and therefore the material undergoes a transition back to the ground state by releasing the absorbed energy in the form of light (photons), heat (lattice phonons) or both.

Materials that have the ability to emit white light have gained significant attention in recent years. A good number of organic and inorganic materials have been reported as sources of white light. They have wide range of applications in display systems, lasers, backlights, indicators and even as sensors [1–4]. Conventional light emitting diodes (LEDs) that are readily available in the market make use of various inorganic semiconductors to emit light. They are highly efficient and capable of converting energy more effectively than incandescent and other traditional light sources.

At present white light emission (WLE) is attained using phosphor conversion method, like in YAG (yttrium aluminum garnet) doped with Ce3+. Yttrium, terbium, gallium or europium, in combination with silicon, zinc or germanium, are the common phosphors used for luminescence emission of white light [5–8]. They consume less power, have good electron mobility and are less influenced by noise. Therefore, these are the most widely used ones for this application. However, as the name indicates, the availability of rare earth metals is limited in nature and their recycling is damaging to the environment. Other disadvantages associated with this method include poor color rendering index (CRI), unsuitability for solution processing and high correlated color temperature (CCT). The main reason for the poor CRI and CCT is the deficiency of emission in the red region [9]. From these points, it is evident that developing new phosphors that are free of rare earth metals, inexpensive, having good solution process ability and good color quality and quantum efficiency are of great relevance and high priority. Organic sources of light are conceptually different from conventional ones as they can provide good homogeneous illumination which are intrinsically glare-free even in compact spaces where it is challenging to use conventional light sources [10]. They have additional advantages over inorganic materials owing to their flexibility, low cost, low toxicity, solution process ability and ease of fine tuning [11]. In spite of the advantages listed above, natural dye-based light sources have the following shortcomings compared to their semiconductor counter-parts. In general, organic dyes as light sources have less lifetime. This is mainly due to the effect of heat generated in the device material during operation. The increased temperature in the device material will cause the natural dyes to disintegrate which adversely affects their light emitting properties. A solution to this problem can be attained by the incorporation of nanoparticles in to it.

Numerous natural sources of fluorescent nanoparticles have been explored for their luminescence properties during the past decade, and their proposed applications are in bio-imaging [12,13], enhanced production of H2 [14], fluorescent probes [15], bio-hybrid LEDs [16] and white light emission [17]. Carbon dots from different natural and synthetic sources also have been explored widely for high sensitive sensors [18], white light emission [19–22] and bio-imaging [23]. As has been pointed out earlier, semiconductor materials are the most suited for WLE due to their band gap being correspond to the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum. Most of the bulk metals which do not show any optical emission gain good emission properties when they are reduced to nano-sizes. Reason for this interesting shift is that as the size of a material reduces, it becomes small enough to confine their electrons to exhibit quantum size effects. This leads to an increase in their band gap which make them show up properties like those of semiconductor materials. Many work have been reported on nanoparticles and inorganic dyes separately and in combination for white light emission, but a very few work have been reported on natural dyes [24].

When nanoparticles are loaded into organic dyes, their light emission characteristics are found to get improved notably. In the same way, when nanoparticles are incorporated into natural dyes, the drawbacks associated with natural dyes could be overcome, leading to improvement in their efficiency. All these improvements depend upon the wise choice of the right natural dye and the right nanoparticles.

Areca nut (fruit of the areca palm with botanical name Areca catechu), was procured from local farmers of Kerala, India. It was washed and the husk separated, ground and filtered prior to use. Spectroscopic grade solvents like methanol and chloroform (procured from Merck) were used for extracting the dye. All the solvents and chemicals used were as purchased without any further processing or purification.

Husk was peeled off from the ripened areca nut, ground and the juice filtered to remove any left residues. 30 mL of the juice was loaded into a 50 mL Teflon coated autoclave and kept at 120 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace. It was then allowed to cool down naturally and the resulted light yellowish colored solution centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min to remove larger particles. The steps in the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from the husk of ripened areca nut is illustrated in Fig. 1. The supernatant containing Carbon dots (CDs) was collected and filtered using Whatman filter paper (0.2 µm).

UV–Vis - NIR absorption spectra of areca nut dye and their derived solutions were recorded with a Shimadzu Make, UUV–Visble- NIR Spectrophotometer (Model UV-3600). Photoluminescence spectra of the natural dye and carbon dots prepared from them were recorded with a Horiba Scientific Make, Spectrofluorometer (Model Fluoro Max- 4). The excitation wavelength was fixed at 380 nm. The monochromator slit width for excitation and emission radiations were kept at 5 mm and the sample was contained in a quartz cell cuvette (1 × 1 cm). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the carbon dots extracted from areca nut juice were recorded with a Jeol Make, 120 kV Transmission electron microscope (Model JEM-2100). Raman spectra of carbon dots were recorded with a Confocal Raman Microscope with AFM (WITEC ALPHA300 RA) instrument having ultrahigh throughput, lens-based spectrograph with focal length 300 mm, and 532 nm DPSS laser. Fourier transform infrared spectra of areca nut juice was recorded using a Perkin- Elmer Make FTIR analyzer in the spectral range 400–4000 cm−1. Liquid chromatograph – Mass spectrometry (LC-MS) of areca nut juice was recorded using a Waters Make, LC-MS instrument (Model Xevo G2 QT) with total run time 9 min and flow rate 0.3 mL/min. CHNS analysis was carried out to determine the constituent elements in the extract. The CIE Color coordinates of the samples were estimated and recorded using Osram Sylvania software.

The FTIR spectra of areca nut juice extract from the husk reveal the presence of functional groups like aromatic alkene, amines and alcohol (see Fig. 2). To be more specific, the peak at 1640 cm−1 indicates the presence of aromatic C˭C alkene group with bending bond. The peak at 3308 cm−1 suggests the presence of amine (N-H) functional group with stretching bond between them. The presence of alcohol (O-H) functional group with stretching bond can be confirmed by the FTIR peak at 3350 cm−1. The presence of these functional groups indicate good water solubility for areca nut extract.

To the best of our knowledge, the chemical composition of the juice extracted from the husk of ripened areca nut has not been analyzed and is not available in any data base. From the LC-MS spectra, the molecular weight of the extract is calculated to be around 323 Da with two main fragmentation ions at 255 Da and 199 Da (Fig. S1). These fragmentation ions represent amide group and OH group which confirms the FTIR result. The CHNS analysis of the areca nut extract reveals that the total percentage of carbon in the extract is 83%, nitrogen is 0.32% and hydrogen 9.52% (Table S1). From an analysis of LC-MS data, FT-IR spectra and CHNS results it can be inferred that the most probable compound present in areca nut extract is sarcosine N-(2-furoyl)-decyl ester with molecular formula C18H29NO4 having molecular weight 323 Da. The structure of this compound is shown in Fig. 3. Sarcosine is a derivative of the amino acid which is present in natural egg yolk and many vegetables [25].

The UV absorption spectra of areca nut extract shows an absorption band at 448 nm as shown in Fig. 4a. From literature, the peaks between 445 nm and 460 nm correspond to sarcosine [25–27] and therefore from the UV spectra the presence of sarcosine molecules is confirmed in areca nut husk juice. The absorption band of carbon nanoparticles extracted from areca nut juice is at 288 nm (see Fig. 4b). It is evident from studies reported in literature [12–15,17] that carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) synthesized from natural dyes have absorption bands in the range 280–300 nm. The variation in the absorption peak is due to the size of the respective carbon nanoparticles.

From the photoluminescence spectrum, we can determine the emission wavelengths and predict the emission color of the natural dyes and their CNPs. In our studies, for all the samples, monochromator slit width for excitation and emission are kept at 5 mm. For the natural extract of areca nut, the emission band is at 567 nm with greenish-yellow fluorescence as the wavelength 567 nm is at the border of green and yellow as shown in Fig. 5a. The carbon nanoparticles extracted from areca nut are excited at different wavelengths and the corresponding emissions plotted as shown in Fig. 5b. It is clear from the plots that as the excitation wavelength increases, the intensity of emission increases and the intensity is maximum when excited at 380 nm. The strongest emission from areca nut carbon nanoparticles is at 460 nm which emits blue fluorescence. The quantum yield (QY) of the prepared nanoparticles has been measured and is found to be 63%. It is also noted that the intensity of emission from carbon nanoparticles is high compared to their corresponding natural dye. The reason for this is that as the size of the particles reduce to nanometer range, there will be less reflection of light as its size is smaller than the wavelength of visible light.

It is clear from the excitation and emission spectra of carbon dots (shown in Fig. 6) that the maximum excitation wavelength for the areca nut CDs is in the range 380–390 nm and when it is excited at 380 nm, it exhibits a strong peak at 460 nm. Fig. 7 shows the TEM images of carbon nanoparticles extracted from areca nut juice by hydrothermal treatment. The average core size of the particles is in the range 4–5 nm and from this it is clear that the prepared particles are carbon dots (nanoparticles with sizes less than 10 nm). The carbon dots (CDs) are partially crystalline in nature, spherical in shape and monodispersed with very little agglomeration.

Raman spectra usually consists of two prominent bands, namely, the D band (D for defect) and G band (G for graphite). The Raman profile of areca nut extract reveals the presence of D and G bands at 1330 cm−1 and 1593 cm−1 respectively, as shown in Fig. 8a. This indicates the presence of disordered carbon and symmetric carbon in natural areca nut extract. The Raman profile of carbon dots clearly shows D band at 1311 cm−1 and G band at 1588 cm−1, as shown in Fig. 8b. It can be noted that there is a downward shift in D and G bands of CDs compared to areca nut extract. The ratio of intensity of D and G bands (ID/IG) gives information about the extent of disorder in the component. The ID/IG ratio of areca nut extract is 0.98 and that for CDs is 1.1. From this it is clear that during the formation of carbon dots from areca nut extract, structural deformation has taken place during hydrothermal treatment of the dye. Fig. 8b shows a broad D band compared to that of areca nut extract, from which it can be inferred that the domain size is decreased and the disorder increased [28]. The integrated intensity ratio (ID/IG) is inversely proportional to the graphitic grain size. The graphitic domain size, which can be calculated from Raman spectra, is about 3.87 nm for areca nut extract and that of CDs is 3.44 nm. This result supports that in CDs, broadening of D band and reduction in domain size has taken place as compared to areca nut extract. Thus, from Raman spectra and its correlated data, it can be concluded that recombination of bonds of areca nut extract has taken place during the formation of carbon dots. When compared to the Raman spectra of carbon dots prepared by Gaddam et al. [29], which were at 1314 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1, we confirm that the nanoparticles prepared from areca nut by hydrothermal treatment are carbon dots as their Raman spectra are almost identical to the other.

The CIE color plot is a color space model developed by the International Commission on Illumination and is known as the Commission Internationale de l′Elcairage (CIE). It is a mapping software that uses tristimulus (combination of three colors, Red, Green and Blue) values and their representative color plotted in a 3D space. CIE diagram is plotted from photoluminescence data and it can give clear picture of the purity and intensity of an emitted color as is seen by the human eye. It is generally represented by two coordinates, X and Y. Pure white light is indicated by (0.33, 0.33) coordinates. In our work, CIE diagram is plotted for areca nut husk extracts and their respective carbon dots, as shown in Fig. 9. The areca nut emits greenish-yellow color with (0.43, 0.47) coordinates and CDs of areca nut extract emits bluish color with coordinates (0.18, 0.25). From Fig. 9, it is clear that the CIE color coordinates of areca nut extract and CDs of areca nut lie opposite to each other on pure white light color coordinates denoted as O. Therefore, we have a good chance to get white light emission by mixing them at proper composition.

In order to confirm the inferences from CIE plots, we mixed the areca nut extract and its CDs at different ratios. They are mixed in the ratios of 5:1, 5:2 and 5:3 and the photoluminescence spectra and CIE plots recorded, which are depicted in Fig. 10 and Fig. 11 respectively. From the photoluminescence spectra, it is evident that the intensity of emission from CDs of areca nut is of higher magnitude as compared to that of areca nut extract. Reason for this is that, as the size of the particle is reduced to nanoscales, there will be much less light scattering. It is also clear that some kind of photo-physical interaction has been taken place during emission from the mixture. The CIE diagram of their corresponding emission spectra at different compositions is shown in Fig. 11 and it confirms white light emission from the mixture of CDs of areca nut and areca nut extract in the ratio 5:2.

As the areca nut extract is incorporated into the CDs of it, the emission color shifts from blue region to greenish-yellow region with a decrease in the intensity of emission. When the CDs to areca nut ratio is 5:2, we get cool white light emission with CIE coordinates W (0.28, 0.34) which is very close to pure white light coordinates (0.33, 0.33), as shown in Fig. 11. The corresponding solution W is then excited at 380 nm under UV light and it emits cool white light as seen in insight of Fig. 12, which confirms WLE, as indicated by the CIE diagram.

In order to study the photo-physical interaction between CDs and areca nut extract in the mixture which resulted in cool WLE, we compared their absorption and emission spectra as seen in Fig. 12. From this figure, it is understood that the absorption band of areca nut extract (485 nm) and the emission band of CDs (464 nm) are close to each other. Since the absorption band of areca nut extract and emission band of CDs lie in the same range, the highest possible photo-physical interaction is Foster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) [30,31]. It is a photo-physical interaction in which transfer of energy occurs between two chromophores where one chromophore acts as a donor and other as an acceptor [24,25]. Here, the dye molecules act as the donor and CDs as the acceptor.

Upon absorption of energy by areca nut extract, the electrons in its valence band get excited to its conduction band. The energy of the excited areca nut dye molecules will be absorbed by the electrons of the CDs which in turn are excited to their conduction band. The final luminescence emission from the mixture occurs from the CDs as a combined effect of areca nut extract and the CDs. The changes in the photoluminescence profile (see Fig. 9) with addition of CDs to the areca nut extract shows an increase in emission at 464 nm which corresponds to CDs. Changes in the composition of areca nut and CDs resulted in the trajectory in the CIE diagram (Fig. 11) that passed through almost white coordinate. If there were no photo-physical interaction between them, the trajectory of the CIE plot would have followed straight path from CDs to areca nut extract as their emission profile would be additive [17,24]. These strongly suggest the presence of FRET mechanism between areca nut extract and its CDs for WLE from their mixture.

Photostability of a white light emitting mixture is very important for any practical application. No significant change in its absorbance and emission and no agglomeration are noticed for a period of four months. Besides, we studied the fluorescence lifetime of areca nut CDs and the mixture ( Fig. 13). From this figure it is evident that there are no significant changes in the decay times of the white light emitting mixture compared to the areca nut CDs. This again proved the photostability of the mixture, which can be utilized for various applications like white light emitting diodes and sensors.

We have obtained WLE from an optimized mixture of areca nut extract loaded with CDs synthesized from it, with good CIE coordinates of (0.28, 0.34), aided by FRET mechanism when excited at 380 nm. CDs have been synthesized by a simple hydrothermal treatment and the size of the prepared nanoparticles from the areca nut extract are estimated to be in the range 4–5 nm. We have used the husk of ripened areca nut to extract its juice, which otherwise is a waste product. Apart from other natural dyes like beetroot, pomegranate or orange, areca nut do not require any modification like pH maintenance to protect it from degradation. It can be stored for a long period of time without any decomposition. The same prevails for CDs prepared from areca nut extract. WLE from such economical and eco-friendly sources is an important development in the area for sensing and lighting applications and can possibly be used in tunable dye laser applications. So we conclude that WLE has been achieved from a single common plant component without any solvents or modifications to it. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time a waste product from a single plant component has been used as a white source of light for possible emission applications.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.10.110.
